pr.title: Split out gamemode-specific files from gm_1B03
pr.author: r-burns

summary: Split a large gm_1B03 translation unit into address-range-based, gamemode-specific source files and headers. The new files cover special melee modes such as Super Sudden Death, Tiny, Giant, Stamina, Home-Run, Fixed Camera, Single Button, Invisible, Slow-Mo, Lightning, plus a later gm_1BA8 range. Build configuration and GALE01 splits were updated so the moved code is linked as separate objects. The decomp.dev report showed this was mostly a relocation of existing matches: 168 new matches in the new units and 168 broken matches in the old gm_1B03 unit, with linked code increasing by 4096 bytes (+0.11%).
what_changed: Shortened src/melee/gm/gm_1B03.c by moving about 1260 lines of later gamemode-specific code out of it while keeping the earlier gm_1B03 range in place.

Added new source files for individual special melee modes: gmsupersudden.c, gmtiny.c, gmgiant.c, gmstamina.c, gmhomerun.c, gmfixedcamera.c, gmsinglebutton.c, gminvisible.c, gmslomo.c, and gmlightning.c.
Added a new gm_1BA8.c/gm_1BA8.h pair for the remaining 0x801BA8FC-0x801BF948 range that was formerly part of gm_1B03.c.
Updated config/GALE01/splits.txt so gm_1B03.c now ends at 0x801B8BB4 and the newly created files own consecutive .text/.data/.bss/.sdata/.sbss/.sdata2 address ranges through 0x801BF948.

Updated configure.py to add each new gm object to the build with per-object matching status: several new mode files are marked Matching, while gmstamina.c, gmhomerun.c, and gm_1BA8.c are marked NonMatching.
Removed a large set of declarations from gm_1B03.h and introduced per-file headers for the split units, including gm_1BA8.h and small headers for each gamemode file.
Added gmscextern.h and adjusted gmscdata.c/gm_unsplit.h, apparently to centralize or expose declarations needed after the split.
Adjusted .clang-format by removing SortPriority: -1 from the static-header include category, which affects ordering around includes like gm_1B03.static.h.
smart_moves: The split follows exact contiguous linker address ranges from splits.txt, making each new C file correspond to a real object-sized slice rather than an arbitrary source organization choice.
The PR updated both splits.txt and configure.py together, avoiding the common failure mode where code is moved but the build/link map still treats it as part of the old unit.
The new files use descriptive gamemode filenames for repeated special-melee patterns while leaving the still-address-named gm_1BA8.c for a less semantically named remaining range.

Per-object Matching/NonMatching annotations were preserved rather than assuming every moved function became matching. This lets the progress tracker distinguish successful object splits from still-unmatched functions.
Keeping moved functions at their original address-derived symbol names, with comments like /// #gm_801BA938 for unsolved functions, preserved searchability and decomp tracking across the split.

The decomp.dev report was effectively neutral at the function level: 168 matches moved out of gm_1B03 into new units and 168 disappeared from gm_1B03, confirming the split rather than a broad semantic rewrite.
decomp_lessons: When splitting a large decomp unit, update linker splits and build object lists in the same PR as the source movement.

A clean object split can produce matching-report churn that looks like equal numbers of new and broken matches; interpret it as relocation if the broken unit is the old source and the new units cover the same functions.
Use semantically meaningful filenames where the gamemode is known, but keep address-range names like gm_1BA8 when the remaining code is not yet confidently named.
After a source split, shared statics, externs, and generated/static headers often need a header cleanup pass; do not leave all declarations in the old monolithic header.
Preserve address comments and address-derived symbol names during mechanical splits so future decomp work can grep old function names and correlate them to linker ranges.
naming_conventions: Gamemode-specific files use lower-case gm-prefixed semantic names: gmsupersudden, gmtiny, gmgiant, gmstamina, gmhomerun, gmfixedcamera, gmsinglebutton, gminvisible, gmslomo, and gmlightning.
Unknown or broad address-range files retain the gm_1B03/gm_1BA8 style.
Lifecycle callbacks keep suffixes such as _OnInit, _OnLoad, and _OnUnload.
Unresolved functions remain documented with /// #gm_801xxxxx comments.
Local helper callbacks continue to use fn_801xxxxx names when no semantic name is established.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Split by exact object boundaries in config/GALE01/splits.txt: gm_1B03.c was truncated to 0x801B0348-0x801B8BB4 and subsequent files cover consecutive ranges through gm_1BA8.c ending at 0x801BF948.
Registered each new object in configure.py with an explicit Matching or NonMatching status to keep build output and progress accounting accurate.
Retained nonmatching pragmas and matching-sensitive constructs in moved code, including examples such as #pragma dont_inline on and M2C_FIELD uses in gm_1BA8.c.
Adjusted include ordering and .clang-format static-header handling around gm_1B03.static.h, indicating that include order can matter for split-file formatting and IWYU behavior.
Used per-file headers with forward includes and placeholder types where signatures were still incomplete, allowing the split to compile without prematurely resolving every type.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the provided slice.
decomp-dev[bot] reported GALE01 linked code at 25.32%, an increase of +0.11% / +4096 bytes.
decomp-dev[bot] reported 168 new matches in the new units and 168 broken matches in main/melee/gm/gm_1B03, consistent with moving matched functions to newly split files.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that convert gmstamina.c, gmhomerun.c, or gm_1BA8.c from NonMatching to Matching.
Search for additional splits of gm_1B03, gm_1BA8, or other monolithic gm files.
Inspect gmscextern.h and gm_unsplit.h follow-ups to see whether shared extern declarations were later renamed or replaced with typed headers.
Look for PRs that give semantic names to functions still left as /// #gm_801xxxxx in gm_1BA8.c.
